title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss the possibility that Lily Gopel is actually either Evelyn Carpenter or Sheila Randall, noting that both women have motives to conceal their true identities. While Evelyn appeared innocent during questioning, Sheila was notably tense; however, Poirot expresses doubt regarding Sheila's claim of receiving an anonymous letter at the Long Grass Inn. Spence voices concern that if Guy Carpenter, a prominent local figure and parliamentary candidate, is found to be an accessory to a crime, his reputation would be ruined.

Poirot analyzes the motivation for keeping photographs, suggesting they are kept for vanity, nostalgia, or revenge. He argues that a photograph of the unattractive Lily Garfield would not be kept for vanity by beautiful women like Evelyn or Sheila, nor for nostalgia, as Lily was unloved. Poirot concludes that because the late Mrs. Upward recognized one of the photos but intentionally misled him by pointing to the wrong one, she likely intended to protect a secret or investigate a connection to Mrs. McGinty’s death privately. This reasoning leads Spence to suspect Deirdre Henderson and her mother.

Meanwhile, Mrs. Weatherby returns home from the post office and collapses on her sofa, accusing her maid, Maud Williams, of spying by rummaging through her drawers. After demanding a restorative drink of milk, egg, and brandy, Mrs. Weatherby is joined by Deirdre, who arrives with her Siamese dog and observes that Mrs. Weatherby appears angry.
